August 28, 1915

Ireland's Record in Athletic Sports

From the Tailtean Games to the Present Time

Including the Complete History of the Rise and Development of the G.A.A.

By "VIGILANT."

XXIX.-STILL IN THE STORMS

The Executive was meeting trouble in every quarter. In some places it arose out of differences with county committees over the appointment of handicappers, or, perhaps, the constitution. Over in Ballina a local sports committee came into contact with the Executive over the appointment of a handicapper with the result that the Executive "proclaimed" the meeting. The sports committee, however, were determined on holding the meeting, and transferred their allegiance to the Irish Amateur Athletic Association. What had just occurred in the West of Ireland was repeated in Dublin almost at the same time.

The G.A.A. was on the handicapper of their own choice, Mr. P. P. Sutton, in opposition to the wishes of the Executive which naturally desired that the services of the official handicapper, Mr. F. B. Dinneen, should be available at all such sports meetings. This difference gave rise to a very sharp cleavage of opinion in Dublin.

The G.A.A. found itself in the Dublin County Committee, and in Cusack and his organ.

The Executive held firmly in its determination and proclamation.

At a meeting of the Dublin County Committee, held at 22 Rutland Square, on July 6th, the matter was fully discussed, and the following resolution, signed by J. P. Nannetti, J. Colleen, and J. Kenny, hon. sec., was forwarded to the Central Executive:

That we the Dublin County Committee of the G.A.A. respectfully ask the Provincial Hon. Sec. of the G.A.A. to rejoin from the proclamation of the meeting of the G.A.A. to the Executive, as we believe that they are acting in accordance with the rules of the Association, as published, in deciding to appoint their own handicapper from amongst their committee, and we further express our opinion that their sports do not come under the jurisdiction of the G.A.A. being principally composed of confined events, with only three open handicaps, and the "proclaimed" sports was awaited with interest. The usual newspaper controversy over, of course, inevitable, and Mr. J. O'Connell, Hon. Sec. to the G.A.A. on behalf of the Executive, and Mr. T. J. Smith, Hon. Sec. to the G.A.A. Club, had to say to the Freeman's column, "The G.A.A. is not to be the target for all critics. At a G.A.A. Convention held in Clonmel, Mr. John Callanan, (Bansky, now M.P.) was elected to the presidency, and at a meeting held at Dungarvan, Mr. J. O'Connell, in the name of the G.A.A., a similar resolution was proposed by it in particular meeting, and resolution, and the G.A.A. was not to be the target for all critics, but, perhaps, enough has already been stated. No man was asked to avow his views on such matters when becoming a member of the G.A.A., and not one of the men branded as "anti-constitutional" can be honestly said to have in any way impeded its progress by the views he had as to the course to be taken in the G.A.A. to ensure the National independence of his country. In vain The G.A.A. was made up of men holding different views, and not one of the men branded as "anti-constitutional" can be said to have in any way impeded its progress by the views he had as to the course to be taken in the G.A.A. to ensure the National independence of his country. In vain The G.A.A. was made up of men holding different views, and not one of the men branded as "anti-constitutional" can be said to have in any way impeded its progress by the views he had as to the course to be taken in the G.A.A. to ensure the National independence of his country. In vain The G.A.A. was made up of men holding different views, and not one of the men branded as "anti-constitutional" can be said to have in any way impeded its progress by the views he had as to the course to be taken in the G.A.A. to ensure the National independence of his country. In vain The G.A.A. was made up of men holding different views, and not one of the men branded as "anti-constitutional" can be said to have in any way impeded its progress by the views he had as to the course to be taken in the G.A.A. to ensure the National independence of his country.
